# The power of AI to automate translation workflows

![The Power of AI to Automate Translation Workflows](https://www.acclaro.com/wp-content/uploads/resized/The-Power-of-AI-to-Automate-Translation-Workflows-600x0-c-default.jpg)

The ability to quickly and accurately localize content is a game-changer for businesses aiming to make an impact worldwide. How are leading companies effortlessly scaling their content production for global markets? The answer lies in AI-enhanced translation workflows underpinned by the people that deploy and support them.

AI technologies are transforming traditional translation workflows into dynamic, efficient systems by harnessing advancements in machine learning and natural language processing. These innovations reduce manual effort, improve quality, slash costs, and allow human translators to concentrate on nuanced language aspects that machines have yet to master. From speeding up turnaround times to maintaining brand voice across multiple languages, AI is making it possible to scale content production like never before.

Here, we’ll explore how AI is reshaping translation workflows, making large-scale global content production more accessible and effective for businesses across [industries](https://www.acclaro.com/solutions/industries/).

## What is a translation workflow?

Translation workflows are comprehensive processes that vary by industry and content type, designed to convert content accurately from one language to another for multilingual audiences. It refers to the entire ecosystem of the translation process and includes both human processes and technology. These workflows typically involve several key steps:

## How AI is used in translation

AI has the potential to make every aspect of this workflow faster, smoother, and more efficient, starting with the act of translation itself.

### The benefits of AI in translation

As businesses seek to expand into new markets, the amount of content they need to localize grows exponentially. [Machine translation (MT)](https://www.acclaro.com/blog/when-using-machine-translation-makes-sense/) has become an essential tool for handling the massive volumes of content required for a successful globalization effort. Here are three key ways AI translation can benefit localization efforts.

## Advanced translation technologies

There are many AI translation approaches, but the two most important ones today are [neural machine translation and Large Language Models (LLMs)](https://www.acclaro.com/blog/how-ai-can-help-overcome-international-business-challenges/).

Both types of AI engines can be used ‘off the shelf’ but can also be customized based on industry-specific or company-specific terminology, offering tailored solutions across various industries. This fine-tuning produces accurate and relevant translations to the specific audience, improving quality and the user experience.

Together, these technologies have significantly boosted viable and effective applications of automated translation, but [some content is still best left to human linguists.](https://www.acclaro.com/blog/when-using-machine-translation-makes-sense/)

## Challenges and limitations in AI translation

Despite these advancements, AI-driven translation still has limitations. To deploy it effectively, businesses must understand these limitations and learn how to compensate for them.

Areas where AI still faces challenges include:

Researchers are continuously working on [improving AI’s ability](https://www.acclaro.com/blog/the-next-5-biggest-challenges-in-neural-machine-translation/) to navigate these complexities, with developments in machine learning and natural language processing aimed at enhancing understanding and accuracy. For example, researchers are working on [ways to overcome the need for more training data](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3587932) for low-resource languages.

## AI beyond translation in the translation workflow

Beyond direct translation, there are a number of ways that AI can be used to optimize the entire localization workflow:

## Global content at scale with managed translation workflows

## Integration with Translation Management Systems (TMS)

AI’s integration into [Translation Management Systems (TMS)](https://www.acclaro.com/blog/how-to-choose-the-right-translation-management-system-tms/) is revolutionizing how businesses manage and execute their translation projects. By embedding AI capabilities, top industry platforms are enhancing their performance, making translation workflows more efficient and intelligent.

From automatically processing and preparing files for translation to enhancing the quality of the final output, AI helps significantly reduce manual effort and increase the speed of delivery.

## Real-world technology, real-world success

Two real-world examples highlight the transformative power of AI in translation workflows.

In one customer example, [a leading commerce platform faced a tight three-day window](https://www.acclaro.com/success-story/sprint-localization/) to localize content into 14 languages within their two-week sprint cycle. The solution? Automated workflows to enable simultaneous language updates, meeting the client’s rigorous schedule.

Similarly, a global eCommerce marketplace [needed to translate millions of words](https://www.acclaro.com/success-story/machine-translation-for-ecommerce-success-story/) for launching in French and German. Through the strategic use of NMT and customized solutions for diverse content types, Acclaro built a fully integrated and automated MT solution that produces high-quality MT translations and routes high-impact marketing assets to our team of vetted, in-county linguists.

These success stories demonstrate AI’s role in enabling businesses to scale globally and improve market connectivity efficiently.

## Future trends in AI-driven translation

Emerging trends in AI-driven translation are set to further transform our approach to multilingual communication. Integrating augmented reality (AR) and virtual reality (VR) offers exciting possibilities for immersive, real-time translation experiences. [Google has already demonstrated AR glasses](https://www.zdnet.com/article/googles-translation-glasses-were-actually-at-io-2023-and-right-in-front-of-our-eyes/) that use MT to translate conversations in real-time, and Meta’s [AI translation model](https://about.fb.com/news/2022/07/new-meta-ai-model-translates-200-languages-making-technology-more-accessible/) is designed, in part, to create virtual reality experiences where language is no barrier to interaction.

We expect these technologies to become even more widely available, cost-effective, and easy to use. This evolution towards instant, accurate translation paves the way for a future where language differences are no longer barriers to global communication.

## Unlock global potential with AI localization solutions

The future of AI in translation is bright, with emerging trends indicating a shift towards more integrated and effective solutions that take advantage of automation in all stages of the translation workflow.  Let’s recap:

Automated translation refers to using predefined triggers to initiate and execute tasks related to transferring text between languages. Here's how forward-thinking businesses leverage it for international success.

![Image 21: Automated translation blog post featured image | Phrase](https://phrase.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/automated-translation.jpg)

The digital transformation sweeping across every industry is steadily pushing companies to automate manual or repetitive processes for increased efficiency and productivity. According to [research by McKinsey](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/operations/our-insights/the-imperatives-for-automation-success), **automation remains a top priority for businesses of any size**—with the trend leaning towards more specialized and granular workflow automation.

[Translation and localization](https://phrase.com/blog/posts/localization-isnt-just-about-translation/) are no exceptions. As more companies seek to expand globally, automated and customized translation workflows become even more crucial in empowering brands to connect with international customers, drive engagement, and improve conversion rates.

This strategic shift **unlocks resources for companies to invest in initiatives that can deliver tangible value to customers across the globe**. To help you make the most of it, this guide explores how automated translation brings these possibilities within reach with the right technology.

![Image 22: Translation memory visual | Phrase](https://phrase.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/Translation_memory.png)

The right translation software for your needs

Drive global growth by automating, managing, and translating all your content with the world’s most powerful, connective, and customizable translation software.

[Explore the Phrase Platform](https://phrase.com/platform/)

Understanding automated translation
-----------------------------------

In the late 18th century, [The Mechanical Turk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mechanical_Turk) captured the imagination of many as one of the first chess automatons. The Turk moved chess pieces for a player on a physical board while seemingly calculating intricate variations in the background. Draped in Ottoman robes, the mechanical marvel concealed the fact that a human was orchestrating its actions.

![Image 23: Kempelen's chess playing automaton known as The Turk | Phrase](https://phrase.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/racknitz-the-turk.jpg)The Mechanical Turk © [Joseph Racknitz/Public Domain](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:The_Turk#/media/File:Racknitz_-_The_Turk_1.jpg)

The original Turk was lost to a fire, yet its resurrection occurred in the 1980s. By then, a human operator wasn’t necessary anymore, and the Turk still exists in a truly independent incarnation—with a chess-playing computer guiding its moves. Likewise, automated translation is the equivalent of giving you the next move in a process as complex as [localization](https://phrase.com/blog/posts/how-important-is-localization-for-your-business/).

**Automated translation refers to using predefined triggers and patterns to initiate and carry out tasks related to transferring text between languages.**

By leveraging algorithms, these triggers aim to speed up the delivery of accurate and consistent translations by reducing as many repetitive or manual translation workflow steps as possible. Automated translation is the secret weapon for growing businesses to achieve impactful outcomes across cultures and languages—without going over budget.

It’s common to confuse “automated translation” with “automatic translation.” However, the 2 terms mean different things:

*   **Automated translation** refers to triggers built into translation software to execute routine tasks, making the overall translation process more efficient.

*   **Automatic translation** is another term for machine translation, which is about converting text from one language to another without any human input.

The toll of manual translation and localization processes
---------------------------------------------------------

For companies on track for global growth, **managing and translating content for diverse target markets can often be challenging**. Sending files for translation, adding translation segments, or making edits to text-based documents or spreadsheets can all slow down a localization project.

Picture this: A spreadsheet where developers paste the source content, a spreadsheet per language for the translated content, and another one for all the terms to be added to a glossary: “Has anyone inserted the translations back into the code?” No one seems to remember: “Let’s send them an email…”

The result is translated content fraught with human errors, duplicates, or even missing segments. From errors and inefficient collaboration to a lack of centralization, **the impact of inadequate automation on translation processes** can’t be ignored. Let’s explore each of these consequences in detail.

Dive deeper

Why spreadsheets don’t work for localization

From limited context and version control issues to error-prone manual handling—discover why spreadsheets fall short in localization.

[Learn more](https://phrase.com/blog/posts/why-spreadsheets-suck-for-software-localization/)

### Ample room for error

Manual translation processes, particularly when reliant on spreadsheets, increase the likelihood of human errors. From manipulating spreadsheet data to copying and pasting content, even a small mistake can lead to significant issues in translation accuracy, version control, and overall quality.

### Inefficient collaboration

Manual translation processes often involve lengthy email threads, a lack of real-time communication, and disjointed collaboration among various stakeholders like product managers, developers, translators, designers, and reviewers. This inefficiency hampers seamless information exchange, contextual understanding, and timely feedback—potentially leading to misunderstandings and project delays.

### Lack of centralization

Managing translation through spreadsheets lacks a centralized platform for complete project oversight. This leads to fragmented data, difficulty in tracking progress, and increased overhead. The absence of an integrated system means missed opportunities for automation, which can streamline processes and reduce or eliminate the need for repetitive manual work. More on that below.

Leveraging cloud localization technology for automated translation
------------------------------------------------------------------

Imagine you need to launch a German version of your website along with an email campaign. This means you will most likely be working with translators, developers, marketers, designers, copywriters, local market experts, etc. Throw in several extra steps they all have to follow, and you can see how easily errors and misunderstandings could creep in.

Similarly, if there are any sudden changes to the original content, you have to quickly update the existing translations. This results in restarting the whole translation process: extracting the source content, getting the translation done, reviewing the updated website source files, and then getting a developer to update the web pages—all of which costs time and money.

To eliminate these inefficiencies, forward-thinking companies aiming to thrive globally leverage cloud-based localization technology: **With a [translation management system](https://phrase.com/platform/tms/) (TMS), handling routine tasks in localization becomes a thing of the past.**

Dive deeper

Localization technology: How to assemble your toolkit for global growth

Discover how forward-thinking companies build localization technology strategies to automate and streamline globalization processes—and learn how you can do it too.

[Explore strategies](https://phrase.com/blog/posts/localization-technology/)

### A translation management system is the engine of automated translation

Being the most advanced form of professional translation software, a TMS combines traditional [computer-assisted translation tools](https://phrase.com/blog/posts/cat-tools/) (CAT tools) and AI-powered [machine translation tools](https://phrase.com/blog/posts/machine-translation-tools/) with project management and business intelligence features to cover all localization needs across an organization.

From file transfers and data processing to quality checks, a TMS automates a range of manual and repetitive tasks with the help of an application programming interface (API) so you can enjoy an [effective translation process](https://phrase.com/blog/posts/effective-translation-process/) and stay focused on what really matters to your business.

* * *

Generating reports and analytics about translation progress, volume, quality, etc.

The real value of automating translation workflows
--------------------------------------------------

With customer preferences evolving and content changes occurring daily, [managing multilingual content](https://phrase.com/blog/posts/multilingual-content-management/) manually can quickly become a formidable endeavor. As your business grows and translation requirements become more complex, manual processes seriously start to hinder progress.

A translation management system addresses all these challenges by providing you with a unified workspace for end-to-end translation project management. Let’s look at how a TMS helps growing businesses reach new markets more quickly by making the translation process more efficient.

![Image 24: Benefits of automated translation workflows graph | Phrase](https://phrase.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/12/automated-translation-benefits.png)

### Reduce errors

Completely automated translation processes minimize the risk of mistakes caused by manual tasks and format changes. Team members don’t need to dig into older files to find the information they need. Automated processes can ensure consistent formatting across languages, reducing the chances of layout or formatting errors that can occur during manual translation. Additionally, automated translation tools can catch common linguistic errors and suggest corrections, enhancing the accuracy of the final translated content.

### Foster teamwork and boost productivity

With a cloud-based TMS, you nurture [collaborative localization](https://phrase.com/blog/posts/top-5-ways-product-managers-can-encourage-team-collaboration/) by enabling open communication, automated notifications, and real-time status updates—resulting in improved productivity. You can organize updated materials without causing chaos within the team and won’t have to worry about response efficiency. A TMS provides all the data you need, and your teams can access the information from any device—at any time. This enables you to consistently keep all stakeholders connected.

### Increase accuracy, consistency, and quality

CAT tools like translation memory, term bases, and style guides help translators deliver more accurate and consistent translations, all of which improve translation quality. With automatically attached screenshots, they can easily understand the context of each translation, helping them dedicate more time to tasks that are more challenging to automate, such as nuances and local variations. This is important because it often means the difference between effective localization and translation failure.

The complete guide to improving translation quality

Improving translation quality is a continuous effort with many moving parts. Here’s what it takes to set up an effective translation quality assurance process.

[See guide](https://phrase.com/blog/posts/localization-technology/)

### Save time and better allocate resources

Automated translation workflows not only trim costs but also free up valuable resources for innovation. With less time spent on workflow management, you can effectively control and [reduce translation costs](https://phrase.com/blog/posts/reduce-translation-costs/). Moreover, by minimizing the need for extensive manual reviews, automation ensures resource efficiency, enabling you to concentrate your efforts where human expertise and creativity are most impactful.

### Improve scalability

If you localized into 5 languages last year, it doesn’t mean that you might not need to address additional markets in the future. Once you establish automated translation workflows, you can seamlessly integrate new languages into your TMS without having to start a new localization project from scratch. As a robust TMS is designed to seamlessly integrate with other software platforms, facilitating continuous content translation and distribution across your digital ecosystem, you will be able to handle more projects and languages with fewer people and resources.

Make the most of automated translation with the Phrase Localization Platform
----------------------------------------------------------------------------

It’s possible to get all of the above—and beyond—with Phrase, a fully integrated suite of translation automation technology that companies worldwide rely on to reach global markets more quickly and efficiently. The [Phrase Localization Platform](https://phrase.com/platform/) comes with an enterprise-ready translation management system (Phrase TMS), a dedicated software localization platform (Phrase Strings), and many versatile add-ons.

To empower business users to make the most of every product, Phrase lets them leverage a whole set of functions that help in customizing and automating translation and localization workflows without the need to write and maintain code.

### Say hello to Phrase Orchestrator

[Phrase Orchestrator](https://phrase.com/platform/orchestrator/) is a drag-and-drop workflow automation builder that allows users to customize, control, and manage the full functionality of Phrase TMS and Phrase Strings from a single interface. The 2 main functionalities in Phrase Orchestrator are the workflow editor and management experience.

![Image 25: Building workflows in Phrase | Phrase](https://phrase.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/03/Orchestrator_Product_Overview.gif)

Phrase Orchestrator lets users automate custom localization workflows with drag and drop from a single interface

#### Workflow editor

In the workflow editor, you can construct simple and complex localization workflows with triggers, actions, and conditions. Every workflow starts with a trigger, i.e., a specific event in the Phrase Localization Platform (e.g., “File uploaded to Phrase Strings project”), to which users can add any number of actions that contain some functionality, like “Create a Phrase TMS project,” to run as a series or in parallel.

Both actions and triggers can have conditions, like logic gates, to determine if they should run (e.g., “Only start a job if it contains more than 9 keys”). Moreover, actions can be set up in series and in parallel, allowing for branching workflows. Once the workflow is complete, the user can publish the workflow where it will sit, wait, and run every time its trigger event happens.

#### Management experience

Management experience enables users to manage, organize, and monitor their workflows. Users can create folders to keep their workflows organized, create multiple versions of a workflow, edit them, and switch between them. Additionally, users can monitor functionality to keep track of workflow performance and errors, inspect every single execution of their workflow, and see if and which problems occurred.

By taking automated translation to the next level and with flexibility worth its weight in gold, Phrase Orchestrator enables you to enjoy customized localization workflows that are tailored to your needs and automated from start to finish. ### 5 Key Trends Shaping the Future of the Translation Industry in 2025

09/12/2024 | Marketing Team

The translation industry is evolving rapidly as technology, globalisation, and shifting consumer demands redefine the way businesses and individuals approach language services. As we head into 2025, several key trends are set to revolutionise the industry. Here are five trends that are set to influence the translation industry in 2025:

![Image 4](https://languageinsight.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/The-vital-role-of-translation-in-global-healthcare-4-1024x788.png)

#### 1. **AI-Driven Translation and Post-Editing**

Artificial intelligence (AI) and neural machine translation (NMT) have already made significant strides in the translation industry. By 2025, AI-driven translation tools are expected to become even more sophisticated, offering higher levels of accuracy and contextual understanding. However, AI will not replace human translators; instead, it will enhance their capabilities.

Post-editing of machine translations (PEMT) will continue to grow as a service, with translators focusing on refining AI-generated translations for cultural nuance, tone, and industry-specific terminology. This hybrid approach will ensure both efficiency and quality, meeting the growing demand for **[fast, accurate](https://languageinsight.com/services/translation-localisation/)**translations.

* * *

#### 2. **Focus on Multimodal Translation**

The future of translation goes beyond text. With the rise of multimedia content, there will be a growing emphasis on multimodal translation, which includes audio, video, and visual elements. By 2025, tools and services designed to provide seamless translations for videos, animations, and interactive experiences will become more prominent.

Automatic subtitling, dubbing, and voiceover translation tools will improve, reducing turnaround times for [**global video marketing**](https://languageinsight.com/services/digital/) and entertainment content. As video-based platforms dominate online communication, translation companies will need to cater to diverse content types across various channels.

* * *

#### 3. **Personalised Machine Translation**

Generic machine translation tools will no longer suffice for businesses with unique brand identities and specific needs. By 2025, personalised machine translation models tailored to individual industries, companies, or even specific projects will become more common. These models will incorporate custom glossaries, style guides, and user-specific preferences to ensure consistency and relevance.

This trend will be especially valuable for industries such as legal, medical, and marketing, where precise terminology and brand tone are critical. Translation providers will need to invest in AI solutions that can be fine-tuned for personalised results.

* * *

#### 4. **Increased Demand for Sustainability in Translation**

Sustainability is becoming a major consideration across industries, and translation is no exception. Companies are looking for eco-friendly business practices, including digital-first workflows that reduce paper usage and carbon footprints. Translation providers will need to adopt cloud-based platforms and collaborative tools to streamline processes and minimise environmental impact.

Localisation for sustainable brands will also grow. Businesses focusing on green products and services will require culturally relevant translations that align with their sustainability values, creating opportunities for specialised translation services.

* * *

#### 5. **Integration of Translation with Other Technologies**

As technology ecosystems evolve, translation services will become increasingly integrated with other platforms. For instance, translation management systems (TMS) will merge with content management systems (CMS), e-commerce platforms, and customer relationship management (CRM) software. This integration will enable real-time translations for websites, apps, and digital communication tools.

Additionally, real-time speech-to-text and augmented reality (AR) translation will see advancements. Imagine AR glasses providing instant translations during live conversations or immersive experiences, these concepts are not that far away from reality in the near future.

* * *

### Final Thoughts

The translation industry in 2025 will be defined by technology, personalisation, and a focus on sustainability. For translators, this means adapting to new tools and refining skills to stay competitive. For businesses, it’s about leveraging these trends to reach global audiences effectively. Embracing these changes will ensure the industry continues to thrive in a rapidly globalising world.

## General Language Statistics

To capture the global markets, one must first know the languages they speak. As an appetizer to the rest of the statistics, here are some fun facts about our world languages.

1. There are 7,151 languages being spoken today, but this number tend to fluctuate as new varieties are discovered or old ones become extinct. ([Ethnologue](https://www.ethnologue.com/guides/how-many-languages))
2. Approximately 40% of languages are endangered. 23 languages already account for over half of the world's population. ([Ethnologue](https://www.ethnologue.com/guides/how-many-languages))
3. Out of all the continents, Asia has the most indigenous languages at 32%, closely followed by Africa at 30%. ([Ethnologue](https://www.ethnologue.com/guides/continents-most-indigenous-languages))
4. The average person can speak two to four languages in their lifetime. ([EarthWeb](https://earthweb.com/how-many-people-use-google-translate/))
5. English, Mandarin Chinese, and Hindi are the top 3 most spoken languages in the world. ([Ethnologue](https://www.ethnologue.com/guides/ethnologue200))
6. English is the most spoken language in the world if you count both native and non-native speakers, while Mandarin Chinese has the most native speakers.
7. The top 5 countries in the world with the best English speakers are the Netherlands, Singapore, Austria, Norway, and Denmark. ([EF](https://www.ef.com/wwen/epi/))
8. The top 5 Asian countries with the highest English proficiency rates are Singapore (611), Philippines (562), Malaysia (547), South Korea (545), and Hong Kong (542). ([EF Education First](https://www.ef.com/wwen/epi/regions/asia/))
9. The most widely used language on the internet is English, accounting for 25.3% of all internet content. The second is Chinese at 19.8%, and third is Spanish at 8%. ([Speakt](https://speakt.com/top-10-languages-used-internet/))

## Global Market Statistics

Discover the behaviours of consumers beyond your home turf. Here are some statistics about the global markets that you can consider while [creating your market expansion plans](https://redokun.com/blog/market-expansion).

1. A large-scale behavioral study in 2014 showed that 75% of consumers are more likely to buy products from websites in their native language. [(CSA Research](https://csa-research.com/More/Media/Press-Releases/ArticleID/31/Survey-of-3-000-Online-Shoppers-Across-10-Countries-Finds-that-60-Rarely-or-Never-Buy-from-English-only-Websites))
2. In one study, 67% of online shoppers said that they have bought from retailers outside of their home country. ([Flow](https://f.hubspotusercontent10.net/hubfs/8483863/FINAL_white-paper-cross-border-shopping-9x9_web.pdf))
3. In a 2022 study by Paypal, 57% of online shoppers say that they shop internationally. In fact, 2 out of 5 global shoppers have made a cross-border purchase in the past 3 months. ([PayPal](https://www.paypal.com/in/webapps/mpp/campaigns/cross-border-ecommerce-trends))
4. 87% of B2C consumers said they would not buy from an English-only website. ([CSA Research](https://insights.csa-research.com/reportaction/305013126/Marketing))
5. Around 40% of internet users said they will never buy from websites that are not in their native language. ([CSA Research](https://insights.csa-research.com/reportaction/305013126/Marketing))
6. 65% of non-native English speakers prefer content in their native tongue, even though they are highly proficient in English. ([CSA Research](https://insights.csa-research.com/reportaction/305013126/Marketing))
7. China is reported to have over 980 million internet users, while the US has nearly 300 million internet users. ([Statista](https://www.statista.com/statistics/265140/number-of-internet-users-in-china/))
8. Translating your website for these 4 countries is estimated to give you access to 50% of worldwide sales potential: United States, China, Japan, and Germany. ([Translated](https://translated.com/languages-that-matter))
9. Translating your website to the following languages enables you reach 80% of the online purchasing power globally: English, Chinese, Japanese, Spanish, German, French, Portuguese, Italian. ([Translated](https://translated.com/languages-that-matter))

10. Overall, global e-commerce sales are projected to reach US$7.4 trillion by 2025, and cross-border online shoppers will play a part in that growth. ([PayPal](https://www.paypal.com/in/webapps/mpp/campaigns/cross-border-ecommerce-trends))

## Localization Statistics

Is your company considering the benefits of localizing your content? Or maybe you're trying to convince your boss or a business partner? Here is a collection of localization statistics you can share with them.

1. Businesses that invested in translation were 1.5 times more likely to observe an increase in revenue. ([Localize](https://localizejs.com/articles/convince-stakeholders-localization-roi-data/))
2. Creating social media content that is visibly localized improved organic performance by 2500%. ([Avensia](https://www.avensia.com/knowledge-hub/blog/posts/stats-february-2022-localization))
3. 84% of marketers said that content localization has helped grow their revenue. ([Unbabel](https://resources.unbabel.com/blog/new-report-global-trends-in-marketing-localization-for-2023))
4. In one study, most online shoppers agreed that it is important for the following pages to be in their home language: product descriptions (67%), product reviews (63%), checkout process (63%), and home page (62%). ([Flow](https://f.hubspotusercontent10.net/hubfs/8483863/FINAL_white-paper-cross-border-shopping-9x9_web.pdf))
5. 41% of marketers say that they localize their SEO for all of the markets they serve, while 40% say they localize their SEO for some markets they are in. ([Unbabel](https://resources.unbabel.com/blog/new-report-global-trends-in-marketing-localization-for-2023))
6. Top of the funnel and brand awareness content is the focus of many marketer's localization efforts. This includes paid ads (46% say they localize these), white papers (42%), email (41%), e-books, blogs, and landing pages/microsites (27%). ([Unbabel](https://resources.unbabel.com/blog/new-report-global-trends-in-marketing-localization-for-2023))
7. Fortune 500 companies were reported to be 2.04 times more likely to improve profits and 1.27 times more likely to generate more earnings per share when they invested in translation.
8. 86% of localized mobile advertisements performed better than their English counterparts, showing a 22% and 42% increase in conversion and click-through rates respectively.
9. In one study, localized copy increased the click-through rates of ads by 22% among women and 87% among men. ([Adparlor](https://adparlor.com/blog/we-ran-557k-in-e-commerce-facebook-ads-heres-what-we-found/))
10. 72% of brand engagement and 66% of content impressions come from localized pages. This happens even when the local page does not have as many followers as the national page. ([SOCi](https://www.meetsoci.com/resources/blog/localized-marketing/5-stats-that-show-the-power-of-localized-marketing/))
11. In a 2017 survery, 94% of marketers in the US and Europe planned to increase their budget for content localization in the following year, making it their top priority. ([Slator](https://slator.com/new-idg-study-commissioned-by-smartling-shows-localization-is-a-top-priority/))
12. 88% of companies surveyed by Lokalise state that they are either already localizing their products or  planning to do in he next 18 months. ([Lokalise](https://lokalise.com/blog/localization-sustainable-growth/))

## Translation Industry Statistics

The need to cross linguistic barriers for commercial purposes has created a surge in demand for translation services.

These numbers illustrate the key translation industry trends of recent years, as well as projections of growth. Spoiler alert: The translation industry has a promising future!

1. The global language services market is valued at **USD 60.68 billion** in 2022. It is projected to reach **USD 96.21 billion** by the end of 2032, with a CAGR of 5.94%. ([Fact.MR](https://www.factmr.com/report/language-services-market))
2. Europe owns nearly half of the global language services market at 49%, followed by North America at 39.41%. ([Statista](https://www.statista.com/statistics/190486/global-language-services-market-share-by-continent/))
3. The size of the US translation services market increased steadily from 2015 to 2022 where it has now reached over USD 9 million. ([Statista](https://www.statista.com/statistics/1280441/translation-services-market-size-usa/))
4. In 2022, the language translation device market reached over USD 990 million. It is projected to register a CAGR of over 10% from 2022 to 2030. ([Global Market Insights](https://www.gminsights.com/industry-analysis/language-translation-device-market))
5. The top 100 language companies have been increasing their market share year on year, but they still only represent approximately 15% of the entire market. This means that 85% of global language services are held by small to mid-sized companies. ([European Commission](https://commission.europa.eu/select-language?destination=/node/9))
6. The translation service market size grew by 40% during COVID-19. ([Market Research Future](https://www.marketresearchfuture.com/reports/translation-service-market-1400))
7. The [machine translation](/blog/machine-translation-software) market alone was valued at USD 650 million in 2020 and is expected to reach USD 3 billion by 2027. ([Global Market Insights](https://www.gminsights.com/industry-analysis/machine-translation-market-size))
8. In 2020, the demand for translation in healthcare increased by 49% due to the need to dispense COVID-19 healthcare information. ([European Commission](https://blogs.ec.europa.eu/emt/covid-19-how-has-it-affected-the-world-of-translation/))

6. On the other hand, there is a declining demand for translation in events, travel and leisure, and the automobile industries. ([European Commission](https://blogs.ec.europa.eu/emt/covid-19-how-has-it-affected-the-world-of-translation/))

7. For a larger view of changes in localization demand, check out the following graph. ([CSA Research](https://csa-research.com/Blog/ArticleID/785/The-Language-Sector-in-Eight-Charts))

## Translator & Interpreter Statistics

The translation industry boom is grounded in the work of translators, interpreters, and editors. Given the cross-cultural nature of translating for diverse markets, it's no surprise the industry has created many freelance opportunities for people around the world.

### (A) Worldwide Stats

1. There are approximately 640,000 translators worldwide.
2. 52% of language service professionals reported that their overall income has increased over the past two to three years. ([ProZ](https://www.proz.com/industry-report))
3. A 2020 survey involving 7,000 professional translators and interpreters found that 75% of respondents were self-employed (freelance). ([ProZ](https://www.proz.com/industry-report))
4. Collectively, these 7,000 professional translators and interpreters represented 178 countries and accounted for 155 language pairs. ([ProZ](https://www.proz.com/industry-report))
5. 66% of respondents in the same survey are females. ([ProZ](https://www.proz.com/industry-report))
6. The biggest challenges translators face include finding clients and dealing with expectations concerning the price and speed of deliverables. ([ProZ](https://www.proz.com/industry-report))
7. Another 2020 survey show that 93% of freelance translators have at least one degree, 39% of which are language degrees. ([Inbox Translation](https://inboxtranslation.com/resources/research/freelance-translator-survey-2020/))
8. On average, female freelance translators charge 7% more per word than their male counterparts. On the other hand, the men charge 5% more on minimum fees. ([Inbox Translation](https://inboxtranslation.com/resources/research/freelance-translator-survey-2020/))
9. On average, freelance translators who are members of professional associations charge 30% more than those who are not. ([Inbox Translation](https://inboxtranslation.com/resources/research/freelance-translator-survey-2020/))
10. On average, freelance translators with 1 to 2 narrow specializations charge 29% more than those without specialist areas. ([Inbox Translation](https://inboxtranslation.com/resources/research/freelance-translator-survey-2020/))
11. Translation agencies are 2.5 times more likely to ask translators to lower their rates than direct clients. 77% of freelance translators have received this request from agencies, while only 41% have heard the same from direct clients. ([Inbox Translation](https://inboxtranslation.com/resources/research/freelance-translator-survey-2020/))
12. In one survey, 52% of respondents said that they have not acquired any formal translator or interpreter certifications in their native language. ([Inbox Translation](https://inboxtranslation.com/resources/research/freelance-translator-survey-2020/))

2. 61.6% of all employed translators in the US are women. ([Zippia](https://www.zippia.com/translator-jobs/demographics/))
3. Freelance translators and interpreters are 55% more likely to work at private companies instead of public companies. ([Zippia](https://www.zippia.com/freelance-interpreter-translator-jobs/demographics/))
4. The employment of translators and interpreters is expected to increase by 20% from 2019 to 2029, which far exceeds the average rate of 4% for all occupations. ([U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics](https://www.bls.gov/ooh/media-and-communication/interpreters-and-translators.htm))
5. According to [U.S. News Best Jobs](https://money.usnews.com/careers/best-jobs/interpreter-and-translator/salary), the salary range for translators and interpreters in 2019 was as follows:

6. From 2021 to 2022, the median annual translator for translators and interpreters in the US is USD 49,100, or USD 23.61 per hour. ([US Bureau of Labor Statistics](https://www.bls.gov/ooh/media-and-communication/interpreters-and-translators.htm))
7. The freelance translation workforce reached a high of 14,362 people in 2020, and 58.2% of them are women. ([Zippia](https://www.zippia.com/freelance-interpreter-translator-jobs/demographics/))

## Translation Software Statistics

[Translation software](/blog/software-translation) may refer to a variety of online or desktop tools that translators, agencies, and businesses use to:

* translate faster with the help of [**machine translation**](/blog/machine-translation) and [**translation memories**](/blog/translation-memory)
* streamline and manage their translation projects
* collaborate with in-house teams or external parties

They are also known as [CAT tools](/blog/cat-tools), which stand for computer-assisted translation tools. If you're in the market for one, check out [this filter](/blog/translation-software) we've created where you can search through the top translation tools of 2022 based on your needs and priorities.

### (A) Translator Use Statistics

1. A survey shows that 88% of full-time professional translators use at least 1 CAT tool. Out of them, 76% used more than 1 CAT tool. ([ProZ](https://go.proz.com/blog/cat-tool-use-by-translators-who-is-using))
2. Among those who use translation software, the 3 main areas of expertise were reportedly **Tech/Engineering** (34%), **Business/Financial** (15%), and **Marketing** (12%). ([ProZ](https://go.proz.com/blog/cat-tool-use-by-translators-who-is-using))
3. Among those who don't use any translation software, the 3 main areas of expertise were reportedly **Law/Patents** (22%), **Tech/Engineering** (17%), and **Art/Literary** (14%). ([ProZ](https://go.proz.com/blog/cat-tool-use-by-translators-who-is-using))
4. Translators estimate that using translation software can boost their productivity by at least 30%. ([ProZ](https://go.proz.com/blog/cat-tool-use-by-translators-who-is-using))
5. In another study, 70% of the translators surveyed said that translation software is essential to their work, with the main benefit being improving productivity. ([De Gruyter](https://www.degruyter.com/document/doi/10.1515/opli-2020-0004/html?lang=de))

## Machine Translation Statistics

[Machine translation](/blog/machine-translation) (MT) simply refers to the use of artificial intelligence to translate content instantly, and it's a gamechanger when it comes to translation speed.

The two most common machine translation systems are **Statistical Machine Translation (SMT)** and the newer **Neural Machine Translation (NMT)**. We explain more about their difference in [this article](/blog/machine-translation-software). Here are some top facts you would want to know about MT.

### (A) Market Stats

1. The global machine translation market has reached over USD 1.1 billion in 2022. ([Unbabel](https://resources.unbabel.com/blog/new-report-global-trends-in-marketing-localization-for-2023))
2. As a refresher, the global machine translation market was valued at USD 650 million in 2020. It is expected to reach USD 3 billion by 2027. ([Global Market Insights](https://www.gminsights.com/industry-analysis/machine-translation-market-size))
3. According to [Global Market Insights](https://www.gminsights.com/industry-analysis/machine-translation-market-size#:~:text=Machine%20Translation%20Market%20size%20valued,for%20real%2Dtime%20language%20conversion.)), the US market for machine translation can be found in the following sectors, and the market size of all sectors is expected to grow in the next 5 years:

4. Asia Pacific is the fastest growing local market for machine translation, while North America currently represents the largest market. ([Mordor Intelligence](https://www.mordorintelligence.com/industry-reports/machine-translation-market))
5. Statistical Machine Translation (SMT) alone contributed to more than 65.0% of the overall MT market revenue. ([Grand View Research](https://www.grandviewresearch.com/press-release/global-machine-translation-market))
6. Since June 2020, machine translation providers have added more than 2,000 language pairs, including many low-resource languages. ([Intento](https://slator.com/press-releases/intento-publishes-the-state-of-machine-translation-2020-report/))
7. According to a July 2020 report, the top performing MT provider in terms of likeness to human translation is Amazon. ([Intento](https://slator.com/press-releases/intento-publishes-the-state-of-machine-translation-2020-report/))

8. Depending on the quality of the MT provided, some translators may charge only 50% to 70% of their regular translation rates for post-editing services. ([ProZ Forum](https://www.proz.com/forum/money_matters/215371-rates_for_post_editing_machine_translation_texts.html))

### (B) Usage Stats

1. Google Translate has over 500 million users daily. ([EarthWeb](https://earthweb.com/how-many-people-use-google-translate/))
2. Over 70% of independent language professionals in Europe said they use machine translation to some extent. ([European Commission](https://ec.europa.eu/info/sites/default/files/about_the_european_commission/service_standards_and_principles/documents/elis2022-report.pdf))
3. 39% of marketers said they use machine translation in their localization strategy. Among them, 83% said they are confident in the quality of their translations. ([Unbabel](https://contentmarketinginstitute.com/articles/global-trends-marketing-localization))
4. Machine translation is the third most used technology by independent linguists in Europe, after translation memory and terminology management. Slightly more than 70% reported using MT for their work, albeit half of them only on occasion. ([Slator](https://slator.com/2022-european-language-industry-survey/))
5. Language service providers reported that the percentage of projects for end-clients using machine translations climbed from 13% in 2019 to 24% in 2020. ([CSA Research](https://csa-research.com/Blog/ArticleID/785/The-Language-Sector-in-Eight-Charts))

6. The best MT quality has been seen in content related to Computer Software, Legal Services, and Telecommunications. ([Intento](https://slator.com/press-releases/intento-publishes-the-state-of-machine-translation-2020-report/))
7. On the other hand, content that is most difficult for MT to get right came from the following genres: Professional and Business Services, Instructions for Use, and Sales & Marketing. ([Intento](https://slator.com/press-releases/intento-publishes-the-state-of-machine-translation-2020-report/))
8. A study assessed Google translations of medical instructions for patients and found that Spanish translations had the highest accuracy rate of 94%, followed by Tagalog (90%), and Korean (82.5%). However, MT is still not recommended for medical application due to inconsistencies. ([ACEP Research Forum](https://link.springer.com/content/pdf/10.1007/s11606-021-06666-z.pdf))
9. The 3 content types that are commonly translated using MT are (1) technical information, (2) client-internal communications, and (3) third-party content. ([CSA Research](https://csa-research.com/Blog/ArticleID/785/The-Language-Sector-in-Eight-Charts))

10. The multinational airline company, AirAsia, uses machine translation to help their 22,000 employees from 16 different nationalities communicate better as some are non-English-speaking. ([Workplace from Facebook](https://www.workplace.com/blog/translation-software))
11. MercadoLibre, one of the largest online marketplace in Latin America, uses machine translation to empower Spanish and Portuguese-speaking employees to communicate with each other and their customers more effectively. ([Workplace from Facebook](https://www.workplace.com/blog/translation-software))
12. A study of machine translation from English to Swedish showed that the output required an average of 3 edits per sentence. ([Recent Advances in Natural Language Processing 2017](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/322032518_Comparing_Machine_Translation_and_Human_Translation_A_Case_Study))
13. The same study found that the human translation of the same text is longer than the machine translation. ([Recent Advances in Natural Language Processing 2017](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/322032518_Comparing_Machine_Translation_and_Human_Translation_A_Case_Study))

## Translation Memory Statistics

Many translation software allows you to save [translation memories](/blog/translation-memory-software) (TM), which keeps a record of all your approved translations so you can reuse them in new projects. The database then suggests translations based on similar or matching segments between your old and new content.

Generally, the more you translate, the more you can save on translation costs by recycling translations because there tend to be more matches as your translation memories expand (here is a [detailed TM guide](/blog/translation-memory)).

1. The use of TM can help reduce the cost of translation updates by 20 to 30%. It also reduced delivery time by 50% on average, resulting in faster turnaround. ([Language Science Press](https://langsci-press.org/catalog/view/106/222/1128-1))
2. The use of TM is most useful in situations where one has a document that is large in size and contains repetitive and recyclable content. ([Monterey Institute of International Studies](https://docplayer.net/14445320-Advantages-and-disadvantages-of-translation-memory-a-cost-benefit-analysis-by-lynn-e-webb-ba-san-francisco-state-university-1992-submitted-in.html))

3. In a 2022 survey, over 50% of respondents say they regularly use TM in translation. ([European Commission](https://ec.europa.eu/info/sites/default/files/about_the_european_commission/service_standards_and_principles/documents/elis2022-report.pdf))
4. The use of TM is highest among translators who translate technical or repetitive texts, as well as those who possess IT skills. ([Dublin City University](https://doras.dcu.ie/17332/))
5. One study reported that 82.5% of professional translators used TM technology. Those who specializes in technical, financial, or marketing texts are more likely to use TM tools. ([Imperial College London](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/228742235_Translation_memories_survey_2006_Users%27_perceptions_around_tm_use))
6. Another survey found that most TM users specialized in translating technical and scientific texts. ([Journal of Specialized Translation](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Translation-and-Technology%3A-a-Study-of-UK-Freelance-Fulford-Granell-Zafra/7600573de132bb0ad0e26c0a8a151a317de34803))
7. The top 3 reasons translators cited for using TM were to save time (86% agreed), ensure consistency in terminology (83%), and improve translation quality (70%). ([Imperial College London](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/228742235_Translation_memories_survey_2006_Users%27_perceptions_around_tm_use))
8. Translators claimed a 10% to 60% increase in productivity when using TM. ([Monterey Institute of International Studies](https://docplayer.net/14445320-Advantages-and-disadvantages-of-translation-memory-a-cost-benefit-analysis-by-lynn-e-webb-ba-san-francisco-state-university-1992-submitted-in.html))
9. Within half a year of implementing a translation memory system, the digital marketing agency, Dataduck, could automatically translate 15% of their content using TM alone, which significantly reduced per-word costs. ([Smartling](https://www.smartc
